prompt:
I need a PR description for a database layer refactor. Here's what I did:

Refactored our database access layer from raw SQL queries scattered across 12 service files to a centralized repository pattern. Every service was writing its own SQL, leading to inconsistent query patterns, no connection pooling, and duplicated WHERE clauses.

The refactor:
- Created a new `src/repositories/` directory with 6 repository classes (UserRepository, OrderRepository, ProductRepository, PaymentRepository, ShippingRepository, InventoryRepository)
- Each repository encapsulates all SQL for its domain entity
- Added connection pooling via pg-pool (was creating new connections per query before)
- Standardized error handling — all repos throw typed RepositoryError instead of raw pg errors
- Removed raw SQL from all 12 service files, replaced with repository method calls

12 files modified, 6 new files created. No functional changes — all existing API endpoints return identical responses. Performance improvement: average query time dropped from 45ms to 12ms in staging due to connection pooling.

This is a large refactor so I want reviewers to understand the approach.
assertions: Description includes a Motivation section explaining WHY the refactor was needed (inconsistent queries, no connection pooling, duplicated SQL)
Description includes performance numbers (45ms to 12ms or similar improvement metrics)
Description includes a Changes section that groups the 18 file changes by concept (repository layer, pooling, error handling, service migration) not file-by-file
Description includes a Testing Instructions section with steps to verify no behavioral regression (e.g., running existing test suite, comparing API responses)
Description includes a Rollback Plan that addresses the complexity of reverting a 18-file refactor
Description includes a Reviewer Notes section guiding reviewers on what to focus on in a large refactor
Testing instructions include a way to verify the performance improvement (query timing, connection pool stats, or load test)
Rollback plan is substantive (not just 'revert the PR') — addresses whether partial rollback is possible or if it's all-or-nothing
